articles.title: Starbucks’s retail arm gets £13.7m tax credit even as sales increase
articles.summary:
Credit can be used to offset future bills as full-year losses at UK division widen to £41.3m and it adds 92 stores

Starbucks’s UK retail arm received a £13.7m corporation tax credit last year, even as its sales increased 6% and it added more than 90 stores.

The credit, which can be used to offset future tax bills, comes after losses widened to £41.3m in the 12 months to the end of September – almost matching the £40m it paid in royalty and licence fees to its parent company.

Designer who left fashion house in January said to be considering options for his 40% stake ahead of talks with lenders

Stefano Gabbana left his post as the chair of Dolce & Gabbana at the start of this year, the fashion house he co-founded with his then partner, Domenico Dolce, has said.

The Italian luxury brand said Gabbana had tendered his resignation, effective as of 1 January, “as part of a natural evolution of its organisational structure and governance”.

articles.title: Battery recycler Ascend Elements files for bankruptcy

Причиной стали отмена государственной субсидии и сложная рыночная ситуация для переработки литий-ионных батарей. Банкротство может повлиять на отрасль переработки аккумуляторов.
articles.original_description: Ascend Elements said that it will be filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in the wake of a canceled government grant and a challenging market for lithium-ion batteries.

Мероприятие предложит демонстрации роботов, обсуждения автономного вождения, кибербезопасности, климатических технологий и влияния ИИ на музыкальную и анимационную индустрии.
articles.original_description: SusHi Tech 2026 is zeroing in on four technology domains reshaping society: AI, Robotics, Resilience, and Entertainment. Expect live demos of humanoid robots, sessions on autonomous driving's software revolution, deep dives into cyber defense and climate tech, and candid conversations about how AI is rewriting the global music and anime industries.

articles.title: Genetics may help explain why results from weight-loss jabs vary, say scientists
articles.summary:
Data on almost 28,000 patients suggests understanding gene variations could improve treatments for obesity

Scientists have discovered how genetics may help explain why weight-loss jabs work better for some people than others.

Variations in two genes involved in gut hormone pathways, which regulate appetite and digestion, may help account for different weight-loss results or side-effects when taking glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP1) medicines.

articles.title: Can we eradicate a second human disease? – podcast
articles.summary: Согласно данным Carter Centre, количество случаев болезни говяжьей воронки достигло исторического минимума - всего 10 случаев в прошлом году. Несмотря на значительное сокращение заболеваемости, полное искоренение паразита остается сложной задачей, ведь только оспа была полностью искоренена. В подкасте обсуждаются причины трудностей и перспективы искоренения болезни говяжьей воронки.
articles.original_description: The number of human cases of guinea worm, a painful and debilitating tropical illness, fell to a record low of just 10 last year, according to the Carter Centre, the foundation set up by the late former US president Jimmy Carter. But despite years-long declines, it remains almost impossible to completely eradicate the parasite. Only one human illness has been entirely eradicated: smallpox. Why is it so difficult, and could guinea worm one day be the second? Ian Sample hears from co-host Madel

articles.title: Sauna and cold plunge: where does the evidence stand? – podcast
articles.summary: Saunas and cold plunge pools are popping up everywhere in the UK, bringing fiery heat and icy cold to a beach, city farm or park near you. Madeleine Finlay hears from Ian Sample and from Dr Heather Massey, associate professor at the University of Portsmouth’s extreme environments laboratory.

articles.title: A common nutrient could supercharge cancer treatment
articles.summary: A common eye-health nutrient, zeaxanthin, may also help the body fight cancer more effectively. Scientists discovered it strengthens T cells and enhances the impact of immunotherapy treatments. Found in everyday vegetables and supplements, it’s safe, accessible, and shows strong potential as a cancer therapy booster. Human trials are the next step.

articles.title: Goodbye colonoscopy? New stool test detects 90% of colorectal cancers
articles.summary: Новое исследование в области микробиома может привести к замене колоноскопии на анализ кала для выявления рака толстой кишки. Ученые с помощью искусственного интеллекта обнаружили связь между специфическими бактериальными паттернами в кале и развитием рака, достигнув точности в 90%.  Этот метод потенциально может стать более удобным и доступным способом скрининга этого распространенного заболевания.
articles.original_description: A breakthrough in microbiome research could change how colorectal cancer is detected—no colonoscopy required. Scientists used AI to map gut bacteria at an unprecedented level of detail, revealing subtle microbial patterns linked to cancer. By analyzing simple stool samples, their method identified 90% of cases, rivaling one of medicine’s most trusted diagnostic tools.

articles.title: Scientists say we’ve been treating Alzheimer’s all wrong
articles.summary: Alzheimer’s isn’t just one problem—it’s a tangled mix of biology, aging, and overall health. That’s why drugs targeting a single factor have fallen short, even as new treatments show modest benefits. Scientists are now pushing toward multi-pronged strategies, from gene editing to brain-cell rejuvenation and gut health interventions. The goal: stop treating Alzheimer’s as one disease and start tackling it as a complex system.

articles.title: Gravitational waves may be hidden in the light atoms emit
articles.summary: Scientists have proposed a surprising new way to detect gravitational waves—by observing how they change the light emitted by atoms. These waves can subtly shift photon frequencies in different directions, leaving behind a detectable signature. The effect doesn’t change how much light atoms emit, which is why it’s gone unnoticed until now. If confirmed, this approach could lead to ultra-compact detectors using cold-atom systems.

articles.title: This superconductivity dies then comes back to life
articles.summary: A strange new kind of superconductivity has been uncovered in uranium ditelluride (UTe2), where electricity flows with zero resistance—but only under extremely strong magnetic fields that should normally destroy it. Even more surprising, the superconductivity disappears at first and then dramatically reappears at even higher fields, earning it the nickname the “Lazarus phase.”

articles.title: These cheap solar cells work better because they’re flawed
articles.summary: Perovskite solar cells shouldn’t work as well as they do—but they do. Scientists have now discovered that defects inside the material actually help, creating networks that separate and guide electric charges efficiently. Using a novel imaging method, they revealed hidden structures acting like charge “highways.” This insight could unlock even more powerful, low-cost solar cells.

articles.title: This new chip could slash data center energy waste
articles.summary: A new chip design from UC San Diego could make data centers far more energy-efficient by rethinking how power is converted for GPUs. By combining vibrating piezoelectric components with a clever circuit layout, the system overcomes limitations of traditional designs. The prototype achieved impressive efficiency and delivered much more power than previous attempts. Though not ready for widespread use yet, it points to a promising future for high-performance computing.
articles.original_description: A new chip design from UC San Diego could make data centers far more energy-efficient by rethinking how power is converted for GPUs. By combining vibrating piezoelectric components with a clever circuit layout, the system overcomes limitations of traditional designs. The prototype achieved impressive efficiency and delivered much more power than previous attempts. Though not ready for widespread use yet, it points to a promising future for high-performance computing.

articles.title: Scientists think dark matter might come in two forms
articles.summary: A mysterious glow of gamma rays at the center of the Milky Way has long hinted at dark matter, but the lack of similar signals in smaller dwarf galaxies has cast doubt on that idea. Now, researchers propose a bold twist: dark matter might not be a single particle at all, but a mix of two different types that must interact with each other to produce detectable signals.
articles.original_description: A mysterious glow of gamma rays at the center of the Milky Way has long hinted at dark matter, but the lack of similar signals in smaller dwarf galaxies has cast doubt on that idea. Now, researchers propose a bold twist: dark matter might not be a single particle at all, but a mix of two different types that must interact with each other to produce detectable signals.

articles.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can’t and it could change medicine
articles.summary: Dragonflies may see the world in a way that pushes beyond human limits—and surprisingly, they do it using the same molecular trick we evolved ourselves. Scientists discovered that these insects can detect extremely deep red light, even edging into near-infrared, thanks to a specialized visual protein strikingly similar to the one in human eyes. This ability likely helps them spot mates mid-flight by picking up subtle differences in reflected light.

articles.title: The world is getting brighter at night but some places are going dark
articles.summary: В целом, ночи на Земле становятся ярче, но эта тенденция сильно различается в разных регионах. Быстрый рост городов, особенно в Китае и Индии, увеличивает освещенность, в то время как в Европе наблюдается уменьшение освещенности благодаря энергосберегающим мерам. Анализ спутниковых данных показывает, что эти изменения происходят быстрее и неравномернее, чем предполагалось, с локальными контрастами, обусловленными различными факторами.
articles.original_description: Earth’s nights are steadily getting brighter overall, but the changes vary dramatically by region. Rapid urban growth is lighting up countries like China and India, while parts of Europe are dimming due to energy-saving efforts and new lighting technologies. The most detailed satellite analysis yet shows these shifts happening faster and more unevenly than expected. Even global trends can mask sharp local contrasts, from war-related blackouts to deliberate reductions in light pollution.

articles.title: Scientists finally uncover why promising cancer drugs keep failing
articles.summary: Новое исследование объясняет, почему перспективные противораковые препараты, ингибиторы BET, часто неэффективны в клинической практике. Оказалось, что два связанных белка, BRD2 и BRD4, выполняют разные функции: BRD2 подготавливает гены к активации, а BRD4 инициирует этот процесс. Блокирование обоих белков одновременно может приводить к непредсказуемым нарушениям в регуляции генов, объясняя неудачу препаратов.
articles.original_description: Cancer drugs known as BET inhibitors once looked like a breakthrough, but in real patients they’ve often fallen short. New research reveals a key reason why: two closely related proteins, BRD2 and BRD4, don’t actually do the same job. Instead, BRD2 acts like a “stage manager,” preparing genes for activation, while BRD4 triggers the final step that turns them on. By blocking both at once, current drugs may be disrupting the process in unpredictable ways.

articles.title: Breakthrough water filter removes 98% of toxic PFAS forever chemicals
articles.summary: Ученые разработали новый метод фильтрации воды, использующий нано-клетки для улавливания "вечных химикатов" PFAS.  Технология эффективно удаляет до 98% этих загрязнителей, включая трудноудаляемые короткоцепочечные PFAS, и сохраняет свою эффективность при многократном использовании. Это открытие может привести к созданию более эффективных систем очистки воды по всему миру.
articles.original_description: Scientists have developed a clever new way to trap “forever chemicals” in water using nano-sized cages that lock onto PFAS molecules. Unlike current methods, this approach can capture short-chain PFAS—the hardest type to remove. Tests show it can eliminate up to 98% of these pollutants and still work after multiple uses. The discovery could lead to more effective water filtration systems worldwide.

articles.title: Scientists discover spice synergy that boosts anti-inflammation 100x
articles.summary: Chronic inflammation often works quietly in the background but can fuel serious diseases like diabetes, heart disease, and cancer. New research reveals that everyday plant compounds—like menthol from mint, cineole from eucalyptus, and capsaicin from chili peppers—can team up inside immune cells to dramatically boost their anti-inflammatory power. While individual compounds showed modest effects, certain combinations amplified results hundreds of times over by activating different cellular pathwa

articles.title: The hidden tradeoff behind today’s most popular weight loss drugs
articles.summary: Новое исследование показывает, что популярные препараты для похудения и бариатрическая хирургия, несмотря на разный механизм действия, приводят к сходным физиологическим изменениям: снижению жировой массы и умеренной потере мышечной массы, что влияет на общую структуру тела.  Хотя эти методы улучшают здоровье, важно учитывать, что потеря мышечной массы может снизить защиту от преждевременной смерти, тем самым представляя собой компромисс.
articles.original_description: Weight loss drugs and bariatric surgery may work differently, but they lead to surprisingly similar results inside the body. Both significantly reduce fat while also causing a modest loss of muscle, reshaping overall body composition. Since muscle helps protect against early death, this balance matters more than the number on the scale. The study suggests these treatments improve health—but not without trade-offs.

articles.title: Your DNA has a secret “second code” that decides which genes get silenced
articles.summary: Not all parts of our genetic code are equal, even when they appear to say the same thing. Scientists have discovered that cells can detect less efficient genetic instructions and selectively silence them. A protein called DHX29 plays a key role in this process by identifying and suppressing weaker messages. This finding reveals a hidden layer of control in how genes are used.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut trigger behind ALS and dementia
articles.summary: Новое исследование показало, что бактерии в кишечнике могут играть ключевую роль в развитии бокового амиотрофического склероза (БАС) и фронтотемпоральной деменции. Вредные для мозга вещества, вырабатываемые этими бактериями, запускают иммунный ответ, приводящий к повреждению нервных клеток.  Устранение этих веществ в экспериментах улучшило состояние мозга, открывая перспективы для новых методов лечения этих заболеваний.
articles.original_description: A new study reveals that gut bacteria may play a key role in triggering ALS and frontotemporal dementia. Harmful sugars produced by these microbes can spark immune responses that damage the brain. This breakthrough explains why some genetically at-risk people develop the diseases while others don’t. Even more promising, reducing these sugars improved brain health in experiments, hinting at new treatment possibilities.

articles.title: Your brain can trick you into liking artificial sweeteners
articles.summary: Your brain might be quietly deciding what tastes good before you even take a sip. Researchers found that simply changing what people thought they were drinking—sugar or artificial sweetener—could dramatically shift how much they enjoyed it. When participants believed a drink had artificial sweeteners, real sugar tasted less enjoyable, but when they expected sugar, even artificially sweetened drinks became more pleasurable.

articles.title: Brain study reveals hidden link between autism and ADHD
articles.summary: Scientists are uncovering a surprising connection between autism and ADHD that goes deeper than labels. Instead of diagnoses, it’s the severity of autism-like traits that seems to shape how the brain is wired—even in children who don’t officially have autism. The study found that certain brain networks tied to thinking and social behavior stay unusually connected in kids with stronger autism symptoms, hinting at a different developmental path.

articles.title: Humans reached Australia 60,000 years ago, new DNA study reveals
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered compelling evidence that humans reached New Guinea and Australia around 60,000 years ago—earlier than some recent theories suggested. By tracing maternal DNA lineages, the team discovered that these early travelers likely used at least two different migration routes through Southeast Asia. This points to sophisticated navigation and seafaring skills far earlier than once believed. The research helps clarify a long-standing mystery about how humans spread across the glob

articles.title: Scientists just found a hidden “drain” inside the human brain
articles.summary: A hidden waste-removal pathway in the brain has finally been caught in action. Using cutting-edge MRI scans, researchers discovered that fluid flows along the middle meningeal artery in a slow, lymphatic-like pattern—very different from blood. This confirms the presence of a previously unknown drainage hub in humans. The finding could transform how scientists approach brain aging, injury, and diseases like Alzheimer’s.

articles.title: Mexico moves to combat pollution following Guardian investigations

Эти меры включают штрафы в размере 4,8 млн долларов для предприятия и внедрение новой системы мониторинга качества воздуха.

After stories revealed high levels of contamination in neighborhood around factory processing US toxic waste, government announces sweeping array of tactics

The Mexican government has announced it will pursue a sweeping array of tactics to combat industrial pollution, from $4.8m in fines against a plant processing US hazardous waste to the rollout of a new industrial air-monitoring system, following investigations by the Guardian and Quinto Elemento Lab, a Mexican investigative unit.

articles.original_description:
Actor Megan Prescott has joined with Adam McKay in the hope that showing bite-size web videos of women undressing will persuade us to save the world. Will it work?

The world, as we know, is in trouble. The last three years have been the hottest ever recorded. Global emissions are still at record highs. The planet is now consistently flirting with the 1.5C limit it promised not to cross. Increasingly, it feels as if we need a genuine miracle to stop us from sleepwalking into catastrophe.

articles.title: ‘Thrash’ Review: It’s Netflix and Chomp, as Phoebe Dynevor Stars in a Familiar but Gruesomely Competent Shark Thriller
articles.summary: Everything in the movie, from the chomping shark attacks that churn up the splashing water with Hawaiian Punch foam to the way that a humungous great white meets her fate at the end, takes an obvious page from Steven Spielberg’s gambits and techniques. But shark movies, because of that derivative quality (and because the directors are not Spielberg), often tend to be dreary claustrophobic affairs. Whereas "Thrash" has a lively competence about it, and a touch of fluid originality in the staging.
